raw_response: {"rating": -1.2, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report presenting historical polling data with minimal editorial prose. The author's own framing choices show a subtle but consistent lean left, primarily through selective emphasis and framing of Republican changes as more dramatic or concerning while presenting Democratic shifts in more neutral or positive terms. The accumulation of several directional patterns warrants a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The section on Republican party transformation is framed around the rise of 'whites without college degrees,' a demographic descriptor that carries negative connotations in mainstream political discourse, while the parallel Democratic transformation section emphasizes ideological self-identification ('liberal,' 'very liberal') — a framing asymmetry that subtly pathologizes Republican change more than Democratic change; (2) The gun control data point is presented to show Republicans becoming less supportive of regulation, framed implicitly as a retreat from a more reasonable prior position, with no equivalent framing of Democratic policy shifts as movement away from moderation; (3) The abortion section notes that 'Democrats have become more supportive of abortion' while adding that 'Republicans and independents have barely moved' — structurally treating Democratic movement as the norm against which Republican stasis is measured; (4) The same-sex marriage section closes by highlighting that 'less than half of Republicans' approve, ending on a note that emphasizes Republican deficiency rather than the bipartisan growth in support described earlier in the same paragraph; (5) The racial attitudes data is presented in a way that frames the decline in Democrats agreeing with a colorblind statement as an implied progression, while Republican stability on that question is implicitly cast as retrograde, without the author offering any alternative framing."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report presenting historical polling data with minimal editorial prose. The author's own framing choices show a subtle but consistent lean left, primarily through selective emphasis and framing of Republican changes as more dramatic or concerning while presenting Democratic shifts in more neutral or positive terms. The accumulation of several directional patterns warrants a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The section on Republican party transformation is framed around the rise of 'whites without college degrees,' a demographic descriptor that carries negative connotations in mainstream political discourse, while the parallel Democratic transformation section emphasizes ideological self-identification ('liberal,' 'very liberal') — a framing asymmetry that subtly pathologizes Republican change more than Democratic change; (2) The gun control data point is presented to show Republicans becoming less supportive of regulation, framed implicitly as a retreat from a more reasonable prior position, with no equivalent framing of Democratic policy shifts as movement away from moderation; (3) The abortion section notes that 'Democrats have become more supportive of abortion' while adding that 'Republicans and independents have barely moved' — structurally treating Democratic movement as the norm against which Republican stasis is measured; (4) The same-sex marriage section closes by highlighting that 'less than half of Republicans' approve, ending on a note that emphasizes Republican deficiency rather than the bipartisan growth in support described earlier in the same paragraph; (5) The racial attitudes data is presented in a way that frames the decline in Democrats agreeing with a colorblind statement as an implied progression, while Republican stability on that question is implicitly cast as retrograde, without the author offering any alternative framing.

explanation: This is a news report presenting historical polling data with minimal editorial prose. The author's own framing choices show a subtle but consistent lean left, primarily through selective emphasis and framing of Republican changes as more dramatic or concerning while presenting Democratic shifts in more neutral or positive terms. The accumulation of several directional patterns warrants a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The section on Republican party transformation is framed around the rise of 'whites without college degrees,' a demographic descriptor that carries negative connotations in mainstream political discourse, while the parallel Democratic transformation section emphasizes ideological self-identification ('liberal,' 'very liberal') — a framing asymmetry that subtly pathologizes Republican change more than Democratic change; (2) The gun control data point is presented to show Republicans becoming less supportive of regulation, framed implicitly as a retreat from a more reasonable prior position, with no equivalent framing of Democratic policy shifts as movement away from moderation; (3) The abortion section notes that 'Democrats have become more supportive of abortion' while adding that 'Republicans and independents have barely moved' — structurally treating Democratic movement as the norm against which Republican stasis is measured; (4) The same-sex marriage section closes by highlighting that 'less than half of Republicans' approve, ending on a note that emphasizes Republican deficiency rather than the bipartisan growth in support described earlier in the same paragraph; (5) The racial attitudes data is presented in a way that frames the decline in Democrats agreeing with a colorblind statement as an implied progression, while Republican stability on that question is implicitly cast as retrograde, without the author offering any alternative framing.
